channel.title: Nature Materials
channel.description: Nature Materials is a multidisciplinary journal aimed at bringing together cutting-edge research across the entire spectrum of materials science and technology. Nature Materials covers all applied and fundamental aspects of the synthesis/processing, structure/composition, properties and performance of materials. Nature Materials provides a forum for the development of a common identity among materials scientists while encouraging researchers to cross established subdisciplinary lines. To achieve this, Nature Materials takes an interdisciplinary, integrated and balanced approach to all areas of materials research while fostering the exchange of ideas between scientists involved in different communities.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Narrow-bandgap acceptors with low energetic disorder achieve over 21% efficiency in organic solar cells
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02589-4

Narrow-bandgap acceptors with minimal energy loss and high short-circuit current density for organic solar cells are rare. Here a designed acceptor incorporated into ternary blends achieves low energy loss of 0.486 eV and high short-circuit current density of 28.82 mA cm−2, resulting in a 21.12% certified efficiency in organic solar cells.
item.dc:title: Narrow-bandgap acceptors with low energetic disorder achieve over 21% efficiency in organic solar cells
item.dc:creator: Jing Tao, Chi Zhang, Qiming Zhao, et al.; last authors: Lingyun Zhu, Zhixiang Wei

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Lingyun Zhu, Zhixiang Wei

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02589-4 Narrow-bandgap acceptors with minimal energy loss and high short-circuit current density for organic solar cells are rare. Here a designed acceptor incorporated into ternary blends achieves low energy loss of 0.486 eV and high short-circuit current density of 28.82 mA cm−2, resulting in a 21.12% certified efficiency in organic solar cells.

item.title: Ion correlations explain kinetic selectivity in diffusion-limited solid-state synthesis reactions
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02596-5

Cross-ion correlations are crucial to quantitatively explain the appearance of intermediate phases and final product fractions during powder synthesis.
item.dc:title: Ion correlations explain kinetic selectivity in diffusion-limited solid-state synthesis reactions
item.dc:creator: Vir Karan, Max C. Gallant, Yuxing Fei, Gerbrand Ceder, Kristin A. Persson
Max C. Gallant
Yuxing Fei
Gerbrand Ceder
Kristin A. Persson
item.description:

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Gerbrand Ceder, Kristin A. Persson

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02596-5 Cross-ion correlations are crucial to quantitatively explain the appearance of intermediate phases and final product fractions during powder synthesis.

item.title: Optical nanoscopy of spatiotemporal metal stripping cooperativity at single-ion and subparticle resolution
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02567-w

Complex coupled ion–electron interfacial processes are poorly understood but crucial to battery dynamics. Here an ion-localization optical nanoscopy that enables single-ion imaging at 50 nm spatial and 20-ms temporal resolution at the single-particle level is presented and used to observe metal stripping cooperativity on Zn anodes.
item.dc:title: Optical nanoscopy of spatiotemporal metal stripping cooperativity at single-ion and subparticle resolution
item.dc:creator: Weidong Zhang, Yilu Song, Jie Zhao, et al.; last authors: Ju Li, Xianwen Mao

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Ju Li, Xianwen Mao

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 27 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02567-w Complex coupled ion–electron interfacial processes are poorly understood but crucial to battery dynamics. Here an ion-localization optical nanoscopy that enables single-ion imaging at 50 nm spatial and 20-ms temporal resolution at the single-particle level is presented and used to observe metal stripping cooperativity on Zn anodes.

item.title: Author Correction: Magnon confinement in epitaxial antiferromagnetic oxide heterostructures
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02612-8

Author Correction: Magnon confinement in epitaxial antiferromagnetic oxide heterostructures
item.dc:title: Author Correction: Magnon confinement in epitaxial antiferromagnetic oxide heterostructures

item.title: To bioprint morphogenic organoids it is essential to chill out and relax
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02581-y

High-throughput and controlled organoid fabrication is achieved by combining 3D bioprinting, matrix biochemistry and stress relaxation in packed hydrogel particles.
item.dc:title: To bioprint morphogenic organoids it is essential to chill out and relax
item.dc:creator: Christopher B. Highley, Thomas E. Angelini
Thomas E. Angelini
item.description:
Authors: Christopher B. Highley, Thomas E. Angelini

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Christopher B. Highley, Thomas E. Angelini

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02581-y High-throughput and controlled organoid fabrication is achieved by combining 3D bioprinting, matrix biochemistry and stress relaxation in packed hydrogel particles.

item.title: Sub-wavelength extreme ultraviolet microscopy reveals domain-wall stability during ultrafast demagnetization
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02583-w

Femtosecond extreme ultraviolet microscopy is used to directly image domain-wall dynamics during laser-induced demagnetization. The domain walls are found to be stable up to the onset of irreversible magnetic switching.
item.dc:title: Sub-wavelength extreme ultraviolet microscopy reveals domain-wall stability during ultrafast demagnetization
item.dc:creator: Hung-Tzu Chang, Sergey Zayko, Timo Schmidt, et al.; last authors: Manfred Albrecht, Claus Ropers

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02583-w Femtosecond extreme ultraviolet microscopy is used to directly image domain-wall dynamics during laser-induced demagnetization. The domain walls are found to be stable up to the onset of irreversible magnetic switching.

item.title: The data-only illusion in materials discovery
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02578-7

Artificial intelligence may have transformed image and language generation, but in materials science, data scarcity and synthesis complexity demand a different approach. Only by coupling artificial intelligence with deep chemical insight can we turn virtual predictions into real materials.
item.dc:title: The data-only illusion in materials discovery
item.dc:creator: Berend Smit, Susana Garcia
Susana Garcia
item.description:
Authors: Berend Smit, Susana Garcia

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Berend Smit, Susana Garcia

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 17 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02578-7 Artificial intelligence may have transformed image and language generation, but in materials science, data scarcity and synthesis complexity demand a different approach. Only by coupling artificial intelligence with deep chemical insight can we turn virtual predictions into real materials.

item.title: Contact-reaction-triggered selective doping
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 16 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02582-x

A gold-surface-mediated doping strategy offers a solution to achieve spatially resolved chemical doping in organic semiconductors, enabling facile control for high-performance organic devices.
item.dc:title: Contact-reaction-triggered selective doping
item.dc:creator: Dongyang Wang, Chong-an Di
Chong-an Di
item.description:
Authors: Dongyang Wang, Chong-an Di

Abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 16 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02582-x A gold-surface-mediated doping strategy offers a solution to achieve spatially resolved chemical doping in organic semiconductors, enabling facile control for high-performance organic devices.
